operations.rationale: Replace the proof-receipt lifecycle table with reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research receipt-state matrix.
content_lines: A proof receipt moves through transport states, not capability states. A theorem-linked receipt names a theorem reference and proof-status scope, but it supports only structural discussion until resolver status is known. A dictionary-bound receipt ties the finite model to a recognized contract family, which makes it useful for receipt design and local surrogate tests.

A fingerprinted receipt has recorded content or normalized parameters, so it may become a replay candidate, but it is still not quality evidence. A resolver-checked receipt has passed or failed a stated theorem-resolution command. A consumer-gated receipt records allowed and blocked downstream uses, and those uses remain scoped to the named consumer.

Workload-blocked is the most important reader-facing state: it means the structural receipt may exist, but workload, baseline, metric, or evidence artifacts are missing, so no quality, runtime, memory, or transfer promotion is allowed. Retired or superseded receipts become historical evidence only unless a later review requalifies them.
These states keep proof authority and consumer authority separate. A receipt can carry a narrow structural fact without carrying a model-quality claim.

operations.rationale: Replace the dense Circle receipt-boundary table with reader prose while preserving exact public-safe receipt facts, fingerprints, theorem IDs, validator boundaries, and non-claims.
content_lines: The public-safe Circle lane is concrete enough to show as a receipt boundary, but not as a capability result. The imported record points to an external Circle checkout at commit `63b0f511`, contract `CC-AI-CONTRACT-ROPE-001`, family `rope_position_distinguishability`, and requested margin `1/328459`. The certifier record says `status proved`, `request_passed true`, `decision passed`, `assurance mixed_theorem_and_computation`, and `theorem_count 55`; the ready digest reports `ready=True fields=31 missing=0 theorems=75`.

The deterministic fields remain narrow: `evidence.d19_proved_request_status="proved"`, `evidence.d19_proved_first_channel_bank_transfer=true`, `evidence.real_phase_dirichlet_witness_guardrail=true`, `evidence.exact_discrete_pass=true`, and `evidence.total_bank_collision_pair_count=0`. The ASI consumer gate checks seven theorem IDs for this receipt boundary: `AIRA-T0058`, `AIRA-T0059`, `AIRA-T0171`, `AIRA-T0172`, `AIRA-T0239`, `AIRA-T0240`, and `AIRA-T0241`.

The receipt also carries replay-facing fingerprints: receipt `91b72a6dcf821a9733f21800cd1093a3d0665588022031ba72c94893800330c3`, normalized request `20e68c5f787e267c6611bc57b8d8e98e1cb0f5a74f272379716a5d83e761407d`, contract pack `df673f8a661fc89a26372685986c92f2221aaa617d6738fce5c2a76bd5d0eeae`, and contract `a0f35d3e89e9b6eac555f0392450f4f75cf7e70f30cff44ec7434f61bd85b468`. The recorded external test batch says `145 passed in 718.24s (0:11:58)`, and the ASI consumer gate rejects digest mismatch, missing required theorem ID, stale contract fingerprint status, and unsupported transfer-claim use.

Those specifics matter because they keep the receipt inspectable. They also keep it bounded. This lane does not prove model quality, reasoning ability, context length, speed, memory scaling, deployment safety, transfer, or ASI. It does not prove deployed proof-contract transport inside The ASI Stack, and it does not promote any chapter core claim above `argument`.

operations.rationale: Replace the dense generated-reader mechanism with a clearer reader path while preserving finite-structure, consumer-gate, workload-evidence, and no-promotion boundaries.
content_lines: The mechanism has one job: let a small structural fact travel without changing what kind of fact it is. A circular-address statement, phase-bank statement, freshness statement, or schedule statement may be useful only if the surrounding system can see its source, its check boundary, its fingerprint, and its refusal language.

The path begins with an engineering object that can be modeled finitely without cheating. If that model is faithful enough, the package attaches theorem references, deterministic values, source version, replay expectation, allowed consumers, blocked consumers, and explicit non-claims. That package may authorize a structural use. It does not authorize adoption, performance language, memory-scaling language, context-length language, deployment language, or ASI language.
```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
A["Engineering object"] --> B["Faithful finite model"]
B --> C["Checked structural package"]
C --> D["Consumer gate"]
D -- "allowed structural use" --> E["Bounded downstream use"]
D -- "missing or stale fields" --> F["Blocked use"]
E --> G["Workload request"]
G -- "baseline and controls run" --> H["Promotion review"]
G -- "no workload evidence" --> I["No promotion"]

The consumer gate is the important hinge. It asks whether this exact downstream layer is allowed to rely on this exact package for this exact narrow purpose. A memory layer might use a freshness package to guard a stale-read case. A cyclic mixer might use a phase-bank package to avoid one bookkeeping error. Neither use becomes evidence that a model got better.

Promotion requires a different lane. Once a structural package requests quality language, the system must ask for ordinary workload evidence: a task, a baseline, negative controls, metrics, result artifacts, and residuals. If those pieces are missing, the package can remain useful as structure while still being workload-blocked.

That split is what keeps Circle-style machinery from becoming persuasive decoration. The formal lane protects exact small facts. The empirical lane decides whether those facts helped. The reader should be able to inspect where the package came from, what it allows, what it blocks, and why support remains at argument unless a separate evidence transition exists.

A useful package therefore makes both acceptance and refusal easier. It should give future systems enough structure to act carefully and enough limits to stop when the requested claim has moved beyond what was actually checked.

operations.rationale: Replace the field-heavy proof-contract interface inventory with reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research receipt fields.
content_lines: The interface is deliberately plain. A formal project names the mathematical statement. A tool run emits the portable package. A downstream layer decides whether that package is enough for its own use. The evidence matrix then keeps the claim boundary visible.

That separation prevents authority from leaking across layers. Coil memory can use a freshness package as a structural guardrail. A cyclic mixer can use a phase-bank or collision package as a diagnostic. Neither use becomes a quality, speed, memory, or deployment result until an ordinary workload has been run against a baseline.

For the reader, the important fields are the ones that make refusal possible: source version, theorem references, deterministic values, content fingerprint, check command, replay state, consumer permissions, failure behavior, and non-claims. If those fields are missing, the idea may still be useful design context, but it should not travel as an accepted downstream fact.

operations.rationale: Replace the implementation inventory with reader prose that names the current narrow artifact boundary without repeating every fixture field.
content_lines: The smallest honest version is not a full Circle transport layer. It is one narrow package that says what was checked, how to identify it, who may consume it, and what it does not establish.

The current repo has that narrow shape. It has generic proof-target and proof-contract examples, a local finite-record model for the receipt boundary, a Lean bridge for the public consumer-gate fixture, and a public consumer-gate check for one pinned Circle RoPE package. The bridge models one accepted receipt, four rejected mutation controls, seven theorem IDs, pinned digest fields, blocked support movement, no chapter-core promotion, and no deployed-transport claim. The validator expects the required theorem IDs and fingerprints, and it rejects missing theorem references, digest mismatches, stale contract status, and unsupported transfer claims.

The next build slice should make the path harder to fake. It should include one accepted package, one stale theorem reference, one fingerprint mismatch, one structural-only consumer gate, and one attempted promotion blocked because no workload evidence exists. That would test the transfer rule directly without pretending that this repo has replayed the whole external Circle project or shown downstream model improvement.

section.title: Beyond the State of the Art

operations.rationale: Replace the dense proof-contract transport target architecture with reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research receipt-field requirements.
content_lines: In its finished form, Circle proof-carrying contracts would act as a transport service for small structural facts. A finite fact could move through the ASI Stack with its theorem reference, deterministic fields, fingerprint, resolver state, replay expectation, consumer gate, and non-claims still attached.

The point is not to make proof language grander. It is to make it harder for proof language to drift. A receipt should let another layer use a structural fact while also seeing exactly what the fact does not establish: model quality, real-valued deployment behavior, runtime efficiency, memory improvement, downstream usefulness, or broad safety.

The mature transport lane would keep receipt states separate from capability states. A receipt may be theorem-linked, fingerprinted, resolver-checked, consumer-gated, workload-blocked, retired, or superseded; none of those states is a measured model result. Stale theorem IDs become residuals, fingerprint mismatches block replay, and workload-blocked receipts preserve structure without promotion.

The book has not demonstrated that transport service. The support state remains `argument` until receipt records, theorem-resolution checks, replay status, consumer-gate tests, stale-proof failure behavior, and downstream promotion reviews show that proof-carrying claims can travel without overstating what the proof stack established.
